bins.name: Power electronics as compliance pathway
bins.desc: Heron supports the ANOPR's co-located generation framework (Principle 3) as valuable but says it describes only one of several sound architectures. It asks the Commission to explicitly recognize energy-integrated power electronics as an equally valid reliability compliance pathway alongside co-located generation.
quotes: why energy-integrated power conversion, not only co-located generation, should be recognized as a valid compliance pathway for large load reliability requirements
The Commission should update its reliability compliance framework to explicitly recognize integrated power electronics solutions as a valid pathway alongside co-located generation.

bins.name: Recognize integrated storage alternative
bins.desc: On hybrid and co-located configurations, Heron argues integrated storage tethered to grid-forming controls satisfies ride-through requirements natively without a separate generation asset, and the framework should recognize that alternative.
quotes: why energy-integrated power conversion, not only co-located generation, should be recognized as a valid compliance pathway for large load reliability requirements
The Commission should update its reliability compliance framework to explicitly recognize integrated power electronics solutions as a valid pathway alongside co-located generation.

bins.name: Performance test over prescriptive studies
bins.desc: Heron warns that prescriptive generation mandates and lengthy studies raise queue timelines, costs, and upgrades. It wants demonstrated performance at the point of interconnection to satisfy stability rules, and streamlined or distinct study pathways for grid-forming and verifiable loads.
quotes: Overly prescriptive rules with generation mandates or lengthy interconnection studies that fail to recognize the capabilities of modern power electronics to support the grid will produce longer queue timelines, higher costs, and more network upgrades.

Facilities that can demonstrate, at the point of interconnection, that their power draw remains within defined voltage & frequency ride-through, ramp-rate and ripple thresholds should be recognized as meeting the AI training load stability requirements the Commission and NERC are developing, without additional prescriptive equipment mandates.
the Commission should create a distinct interconnection treatment pathway for large loads that demonstrate grid-forming capability

RTOs and transmission providers should be directed to develop streamlined interconnection study procedures for loads implementing open, publicly documented control architectures, reducing the study depth required for facilities whose control behavior is fully verifiable.

bins.name: Symmetric cost causation and benefits
bins.desc: Heron argues performance-based rules lower costs for ratepayers, and that cost-causation under Principle 8 should run symmetrically so grid-forming loads' system benefits are credited against direct-assignment network upgrade costs.
quotes: Overly prescriptive rules with generation mandates or lengthy interconnection studies that fail to recognize the capabilities of modern power electronics to support the grid will produce longer queue timelines, higher costs, and more network upgrades.
Where energy-integrated power electronics can satisfy the same performance requirements at lower cost, ratepayers benefit.
the Commission should direct RTOs and transmission owners to credit the grid services provided by grid-forming large loads against any direct-assignment network upgrade costs.
The cost-causation principle underlying ANOPR Principle 8 should apply symmetrically to benefits as well as costs.

bins.name: Extend flexible-load treatment to grid-forming
bins.desc: Heron asks the Commission to distinguish loads with integrated ride-through and load-smoothing, and to extend the expedited treatment being considered for flexible and curtailable loads to grid-forming loads.
quotes: The Commission's evolving reliability framework should distinguish between data centers designed with integrated ride-through and load-smoothing capability and those without.
The expedited study pathway the Commission is considering for flexible and curtailable loads is a natural starting point; grid-forming loads should qualify for comparable treatment.

bins.name: Expedited path for low-burden loads
bins.desc: Heron supports expedited interconnection study for grid-forming loads and preferential queue treatment for facilities with open, verifiable controls, on the basis that they impose materially lower study burdens.
quotes: The expedited study pathway the Commission is considering for flexible and curtailable loads is a natural starting point; grid-forming loads should qualify for comparable treatment.

The Commission should also explore preferential queue treatment or reduced study deposit requirements for such facilities, recognizing that the study burden those facilities impose on operators is materially lower.

bins.name: Credit benefits against upgrade costs
bins.desc: Heron asks RTOs and transmission owners to credit the grid services of grid-forming loads against direct-assignment network upgrade costs, treating avoided synchronous condensers or flicker devices as a quantifiable benefit to the system.
quotes: the Commission should direct RTOs and transmission owners to credit the grid services provided by grid-forming large loads against any direct-assignment network upgrade costs.
The cost-causation principle underlying ANOPR Principle 8 should apply symmetrically to benefits as well as costs.

bins.name: Reduced study deposits for verifiable loads
bins.desc: Heron asks the Commission to explore reduced study deposit requirements for facilities whose open, documented control behavior lowers the study burden on operators.
quotes: The Commission should also explore preferential queue treatment or reduced study deposit requirements for such facilities, recognizing that the study burden those facilities impose on operators is materially lower.

bins.name: Ride-through and load-smoothing design
bins.desc: Heron argues datacenter network risk is an artifact of legacy power architecture, not inherent, and that facilities demonstrating integrated ride-through, ramp-rate, and ripple control at the point of interconnection should be recognized as meeting stability requirements without additional equipment mandates.
quotes: The Commission's evolving reliability framework should distinguish between data centers designed with integrated ride-through and load-smoothing capability and those without.

Facilities that can demonstrate, at the point of interconnection, that their power draw remains within defined voltage & frequency ride-through, ramp-rate and ripple thresholds should be recognized as meeting the AI training load stability requirements the Commission and NERC are developing, without additional prescriptive equipment mandates.

bins.name: Grid-forming loads as grid asset
bins.desc: Heron contends a grid-forming large load is a positive grid asset that provides voltage support, inertial response, and harmonic damping. It urges a distinct interconnection treatment pathway recognizing those contributions.
quotes: A large load equipped with grid-forming controls is not merely a less-harmful version of a passive load but can be a positive grid asset.
the Commission should create a distinct interconnection treatment pathway for large loads that demonstrate grid-forming capability

bins.name: Open verifiable controls cut study friction
bins.desc: Heron argues opaque black-box inverter models drive bespoke EMT studies and queue backlog, and that open, publicly documented control architectures (its OpenVSM) should earn streamlined studies and reduced study depth because behavior is fully verifiable.
quotes: RTOs and transmission providers should be directed to develop streamlined interconnection study procedures for loads implementing open, publicly documented control architectures, reducing the study depth required for facilities whose control behavior is fully verifiable.

The Commission should also explore preferential queue treatment or reduced study deposit requirements for such facilities, recognizing that the study burden those facilities impose on operators is materially lower.

bins.name: Technology-neutral performance standards
bins.desc: Heron's central ask is performance-based, technology-neutral standards that specify required grid behaviors at the point of interconnection without dictating equipment categories, leaving how to meet them to developers.
quotes: The relevant test should be performance-based: does the facility meet specified ride-through duration, ramp-rate, fault current, and voltage stability requirements at the point of interconnection?
The Commission should write performance-based standards that specify what grid behaviors large loads must exhibit ... without specifying which equipment categories must be deployed to achieve them.

bins.name: Standards shape domestic manufacturing
bins.desc: Heron argues FERC's technical standards determine which equipment is bought and where, and that technology-neutral performance standards create the stable demand signal U.S. advanced power electronics manufacturers need to invest in factory capacity.
quotes: it creates the stable, technology-neutral demand signal that domestic manufacturers need to justify the capital investment required to build factory capacity at scale.
